subject: ap-world-history
theme: Transoceanic Interconnections, 1450–1750: European maritime expansion creates the first truly global trade networks, with profound and often devastating consequences for indigenous peoples and enslaved Africans.

core_concepts.title: European Maritime Exploration: Causes and Technology
core_concepts.summary: European expansion in the 15th–16th centuries had multiple drivers: desire to bypass Muslim and Italian intermediaries in Asian spice trade, Christian missionary zeal, individual ambition for wealth and glory, and competitive state rivalry. Portuguese improvements in navigation technology — the caravel (a maneuverable ship with both lateen and square sails), the astrolabe, and use of the magnetic compass — enabled sustained Atlantic and Indian Ocean voyaging. Portuguese explorers reached West Africa (1440s), rounded the Cape of Good Hope (Dias, 1488), reached India (da Gama, 1498), and established trading posts in the Indian Ocean.

core_concepts.title: Spanish Conquest of the Americas
core_concepts.summary: Columbus's 1492 landing in the Caribbean initiated Spanish conquest of the Americas. Hernán Cortés defeated the Aztec Empire (1519–1521) with a coalition of indigenous allies resentful of Aztec tribute demands, combined with smallpox epidemics that devastated the population. Francisco Pizarro conquered the Inca Empire (1532–1535), also aided by indigenous allies and disease. The Spanish established a colonial system based on silver mining (Potosí, Peru became the world's largest silver producer), the encomienda (grants of indigenous labor to Spanish colonizers), and the Catholic Church's missionary role.

core_concepts.title: The Columbian Exchange
core_concepts.summary: The Columbian Exchange — the transfer of plants, animals, and diseases between the Eastern and Western Hemispheres after 1492 — was the most consequential biological event since the Ice Ages. From Americas to Eurasia/Africa: maize, potatoes, tomatoes, cacao, tobacco, and rubber transformed Eurasian diets and population growth. From Eurasia/Africa to Americas: wheat, rice, horses, cattle, pigs, and crucially, smallpox, measles, and influenza. Indigenous American populations, having no prior exposure, suffered catastrophic mortality estimated at 50–90% within a century of contact — the worst demographic collapse in human history.

core_concepts.title: The Atlantic Slave Trade
core_concepts.summary: European demand for plantation labor in the Americas — after indigenous labor collapsed from disease — created the transatlantic slave trade. Between 1500 and 1900, approximately 12.5 million Africans were forcibly transported across the Atlantic, with roughly 10.7 million surviving the Middle Passage. The brutal voyage involved enslaved people chained below decks in horrific conditions with 10–15% mortality rates. Brazil received the most enslaved Africans (~4.9 million); the Caribbean another ~4 million. The slave trade devastated West and Central African societies, disrupting political stability and generating internal African slave raids.

core_concepts.title: Colonial Labor Systems and Social Hierarchies
core_concepts.summary: Spanish colonial America developed complex systems of extraction and stratification. The encomienda granted colonizers rights to indigenous labor in exchange for supposed protection and Christianization. The mita — adapted from an Inca state labor draft — compelled indigenous men to work in silver mines like Potosí, where dangerous conditions and mercury exposure caused mass mortality. A rigid caste system (casta) emerged: peninsulares (Spain-born), criollos (American-born Europeans), mestizos (mixed European-indigenous), mulatos (mixed European-African), and indigenous and African-descended people at the bottom. Catholic missionaries sometimes challenged but more often participated in colonial hierarchies.

core_concepts.title: The Portuguese and Dutch Trading Empires
core_concepts.summary: The Portuguese Estado da India was a trading-post empire controlling key nodes in the Indian Ocean network through naval power, disrupting Arab merchant dominance but never fully controlling trade. The Dutch East India Company (VOC, 1602) was the world's first joint-stock company with quasi-governmental powers — it could declare war, mint coins, and sign treaties. The VOC expelled the Portuguese from most Asian trade posts and established a monopoly on spices from the Banda Islands (today's Indonesia), using violent coercion including massacres. The joint-stock company model allowed pooling capital and sharing risk — a major financial innovation.

key_terms.definition: The transfer of plants, animals, diseases, and peoples between the Eastern Hemisphere (Eurasia, Africa) and the Western Hemisphere (Americas) following Columbus's 1492 voyage.
Spanish colonial grant giving a colonizer the right to extract labor and tribute from a specified number of indigenous people in exchange for their supposed 'protection' and Christianization.
Spanish colonial forced labor draft adapted from the Inca system, compelling indigenous men to work in mines and on public projects for a portion of each year.
Silver mining city in modern Bolivia, the largest source of silver in the colonial world; the mine's brutal conditions under the mita system caused massive indigenous mortality.
The horrific transatlantic voyage endured by enslaved Africans, crossing the Atlantic in overcrowded ships with 10–15% mortality rates from disease, dehydration, and abuse.
Three-way Atlantic trade pattern: European manufactured goods to Africa; enslaved Africans to Americas; American commodities (sugar, tobacco, silver) back to Europe.
Spanish colonial racial hierarchy categorizing people by ancestry: peninsulares (Spain-born) > criollos (American-born Europeans) > mestizos > mulatos > indigenous/African.
Dutch joint-stock company (1602–1799) that monopolized Asian spice trade, controlled territories, and was effectively a colonial power in its own right.
Business organization that issues shares to investors who provide capital and share in profits and losses — innovation that pooled resources for expensive long-distance trade ventures.
Portuguese 'State of India' — a trading-post empire of fortified ports in the Indian Ocean, using naval power to control key trade nodes.
Spanish priest and missionary who documented and protested the violent mistreatment of indigenous peoples under Spanish colonialism, advocating for their legal protections.
Portuguese sailing ship combining lateen and square sails, enabling Atlantic exploration; maneuverable enough to sail against winds that defeated earlier vessel designs.
Spanish treasure ships sailing annually between Manila (Philippines) and Acapulco (Mexico), connecting Asian and American trade and circulating Potosí silver to China.
Blending of two or more religious traditions; common in colonial Americas where indigenous peoples merged Catholic and traditional religious practices.
Economic theory that a nation's wealth derives from accumulating precious metals through favorable trade balance; justified colonial extraction as enriching the imperial 'mother country.'

exam_tip: Unit 4 generates some of the most common SAQ and DBQ topics on the AP exam. Master the argument that the Columbian Exchange had radically different effects in the Eastern vs. Western Hemisphere — catastrophic for indigenous Americans through disease, beneficial for Eurasian diets through new food crops. For the slave trade, always emphasize African agency is not eliminated — African rulers participated in capturing and selling enslaved people — even while stressing the overwhelming coercive violence of the system. Know the difference between encomienda (labor grants to colonizers) and mita (state-mandated labor draft).
